chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q1_analysis: The paper describes the implementation details and evaluation setup but does not provide explicit working links to public repositories or datasets for its protocols (Simple Moonshot, Pipelined Moonshot, Commit Moonshot). It references a modified code base from Facebook Research [32], but this is not presented as an open artifact link.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q2_analysis: The evaluation uses physical testbeds: nodes are distributed across five distinct AWS regions (us-east-1, us-west-1, eu-north-1, ap-northeast-1, ap-southeast-2) and run on dedicated EC2 instances connected via point-to-point links. This constitutes a real hardware deployment/wide-area network testbed.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q3_analysis: The paper provides clear, explicit definitions for the system model (Byzantine fault tolerance with $f < n/3$) and defines specific properties like 'Reorg Resilience' (Definition 5) and discusses the underlying assumptions of the partially synchronous network model ($\delta \le \Delta$ after GST). The threat model is clearly defined as a static, computationally bounded adversary corrupting up to $f$ nodes.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q4_analysis: The paper provides rigorous, data-driven analysis of trade-offs. It compares throughput and latency across different configurations (varying network size, varying payload sizes) against Jolteon. Specifically, it quantifies the performance gains/losses related to pipelining versus explicit commit phases (e.g., comparing PM vs CM in Section VI). The theoretical section also analyzes overhead implications of pipelining.

major_weakness: The paper lacks explicit working links to public repositories or datasets necessary for full reproducibility.
